metadata.summary: Kanban and SAFe ceremonies: daily standup, weekly replenishment/SDR, bi-weekly retro, monthly strategy. Use throughput + cycle time for forecasting instead of velocity.

content.title: Kanban and Scaled Agile Ceremonies
content.summary: Alternative agile cadences for continuous-flow teams (Kanban) and multi-team programs (SAFe). Kanban replaces fixed sprints with five cadences: daily standup (walk-the-board right-to-left), weekly replenishment, weekly service-delivery review, bi-weekly retrospective, and monthly strategy review. SAFe adds PI Planning (2-day, every 8-12 weeks), Scrum-of-Scrums (daily), PO Sync (weekly), and Inspect-and-Adapt (end of PI). The rule: throughput plus 85th-percentile cycle time beats velocity for forecasting; use Monte Carlo on these for date predictions.

why.p: Sprint boundaries break down for ops, platform, and support teams with high mid-sprint volatility. Kanban cadences decouple commitment from timebox, making WIP limits the primary flow-control mechanism. At scale (3+ Scrum teams), PI Planning provides the cross-team dependency surface that individual standups cannot — it is the SAFe ceremony that delivers the most value per hour spent.

list.item: Continuous-flow teams (support, ops, data engineering) with no natural sprint boundary.
Multi-team programs needing PI Planning and Scrum-of-Scrums coordination.
Teams with high mid-sprint volatility (incident-heavy ops, content production).
Platform teams serving many internal customers where replenishment fits better than sprint commitment.
Hybrid ScrumBan setups: sprint planning kept for steering, WIP limits enforced for flow.
A single team of fewer than 8 people with stable feature work — Scrum's ceremonies suffice.
Pure project work with fixed end date and known scope — predictive cadences fit better.
Organizations drowning in ceremonies; adding SAFe events without removing others causes revolt.
Discovery-heavy product work where dual-track agile maps better than SAFe.
Solopreneur or 2-person teams — overhead exceeds value; a personal Kanban board is enough.

section.name: kanban-cadences
section.p: Kanban ceremonies focus on flow, WIP management, and continuous improvement rather than time-boxed iterations.
section.term: Daily walk-the-board meeting, right-to-left. Start with blocked/at-risk items, then move blockers. Owners speak only for their work. 15 minutes max. Uses WIP visualization to discuss flow constraints.

PO + tech lead select top-N "Ready" items from the backlog (prioritized by value × age × dependency). Ordered list becomes the next week's pull sequence. 30 minutes. No "anyone interested" — focus is selection, not discussion.

Weekly metrics review: throughput (items/week, last 4 weeks), cycle time (avg and 85th percentile per state), WIP vs limits, list of aged items (>2x median), blockers. Data-driven, not status theater. Drives policy changes (WIP limit adjustments, priority re-ranking, process updates). 45 minutes.

Discuss impediments, WIP limit breaches, outdated process steps, and team growth. Owners: process improvements and people development. Output: 2-3 actions with owners and due dates. Not a complaint session.
Leadership alignment: roadmap progress vs plan, new strategic direction (if any), resource constraints, customer feedback themes. Drives replenishment priority for the next month. 60 minutes.

rule.statement: Enforce WIP limits as a first-class column setting with a hard block (Jira Premium / Linear Cycles); soft warnings are ignored under pressure.
rule.rationale: WIP limits without enforcement become aspirational. Teams blow past them under pressure without visible feedback; hard blocks force the conversation about priority.

rule.statement: Throughput + 85th-percentile cycle time beats velocity for forecasting; use Monte Carlo simulation on these metrics for date predictions.
rule.rationale: Velocity is a Scrum artifact dependent on sprint length and planning accuracy. Kanban teams deliver on a sliding window; cycle-time percentiles are more stable and actionable for forecasting.

section.name: scaled-agile-framework
section.p: SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) layers Kanban+Scrum ceremonies with Program Increment (PI) planning and cross-team synchronization.
section.term: 8-12 week planning cycle. Each PI is split into 2 iterations (each ~2 weeks). Starts with PI Planning, ends with Inspect-and-Adapt.

2-day, in-person (preferred) quarterly event. All teams (3+) attend with their PO and tech lead. Day 1: Product vision, capacity planning, team breakouts (each team plans stories into iterations). Day 2: cross-team dependency board (identify and resolve blockers), confidence vote. Output: PI roadmap, capacity sheet, dependency board.
Daily 15-min standup of team representatives to discuss cross-team dependencies, risks, escalations. Not a status round-robin — focus is dependency resolution.
Weekly 1-hour sync of all POs to align on priority, roadmap, and customer feedback. Feeds into next replenishment.

End-of-PI meeting (~90 min). Program-level retrospective: measure objectives + key results (OKRs) achieved, systemic impediments, process improvements. Output: action items with owners + due dates for next PI. Items tracked in the program backlog.

rule.statement: Lock the PI Planning agenda exactly the day before; teams that "tweak" it lose 30+ minutes to confusion.
rule.rationale: PI Planning thrives on predictability. Late agenda changes ripple through breakout groups and reduce decision quality.

rule.statement: Track I&A actions in the same backlog as features with owners and due dates; otherwise they get prioritized to zero and re-surface every PI.
rule.rationale: Improvements without tracking disappear into noise. Make them first-class work items.

antipattern.description: WIP limits without leadership backing become aspirational; teams blow past them under pressure.
antipattern.reason: Without visible enforcement and leadership support for pushing back on demand, WIP limits are ignored.

antipattern.description: Service-delivery review devolves into status theater unless metrics drive concrete policy changes.
antipattern.reason: SDR without decisions becomes a data dump. Tie every metric to an action (WIP adjustment, priority re-rank, process change).

antipattern.description: PI Planning quality drops sharply when run remote-only with poor tooling (Miro lag, audio chaos).
antipattern.reason: PI Planning depends on real-time cross-team problem-solving. Remote logistics must match in-person (clear tool, low latency, breakout rooms).

antipattern.description: Scrum-of-Scrums turns into a status round-robin instead of a dependency-resolution forum.
antipattern.reason: SoS without focused agenda becomes theater. Set rule: only discuss cross-team dependencies and escalations; status updates happen in team standups.

section.name: metrics-and-flow
section.p: Kanban metrics provide visibility into flow and enable data-driven process changes.

rule.statement: Kanban metrics (cycle time, throughput) need ≥6 weeks of clean data before they are trustworthy for forecasting.
rule.rationale: Short windows capture noise (one slow item, holiday week). Six-week minimum smooths anomalies and reveals true process stability.

section.term: Time from "In Progress" to "Done" per issue. Median and 85th percentile tell the whole story. Use for forecasting and SLA promises.
Time from "Created" to "Done". Includes waiting in backlog. Use for customer communication ("how long until my request is done?").
Items completed per week (or per sprint). Stable measure of team capacity. Use for roadmap planning and Monte Carlo forecasting.

example.title: Service-delivery review dashboard
example.code:
## Service Delivery Review — Week of 2026-04-28
### Flow Metrics - Throughput (28d): 18 items - Weekly avg: 4.5 items/week - Cycle time (median): 3.2 days - Cycle time (p85): 7.1 days
### WIP Status | Column | WIP Limit | Current | Status | |-------------|-----------|---------|--------| | Backlog | ∞ | 42 | OK | | Ready | 10 | 8 | OK | | In Progress | 5 | 5 | AT CAP | | Code Review | 3 | 2 | OK | | QA | 3 | 3 | AT CAP | | Done | — | — | — |
### Aged Items (>2x median cycle time = >6.4 days) - PROJ-204: Bug, "In Progress" since Apr 20 — owner: @alice - PROJ-198: Feature, "Code Review" since Apr 19 — blocker: waiting for 3rd reviewer
### Actions 1. (@alice) Unblock PROJ-204 by EOD Thursday or escalate. 2. (@bob) Hire code-review signal for PROJ-198 or adjust reviewer rota.

section.name: agentic-workflows
section.p: Agent-driven ceremony support automates agenda prep, metrics calculation, action tracking, and facilitation.

list.item: kanban-flow-analyst — pulls cycle/lead time, throughput, WIP from API; flags outliers, aging items.
replenishment-prepper — orders the "Ready" queue by priority × age × dependency, returns top-N picks.
pi-planning-facilitator — generates the PI agenda, capacity sheet, dependency board template per team.
inspect-adapt-tracker — captures I&A action items into the program backlog with owners + due dates.
/faion — already in repo; can be invoked to walk SDD tasks once they are committed in PI plan.

example.title: Flow analyst prompt
example.code: You are kanban-flow-analyst. Inputs: JSON list of issues with state, entered_state_at, exited_state_at, wip_limit per column. Output: Markdown brief with throughput (items/week, last 4 weeks), avg cycle time per state, WIP vs limit, list of items aged > 2x median cycle time. Do not infer reasons — only report numbers.

antipattern.description: Agents trained on Scrum default to sprint-thinking; explicitly tell them "this team uses Kanban, no sprints, WIP-limited."
antipattern.reason: LLM training data skews Scrum-heavy. Without explicit constraint, agents suggest sprint boundaries and velocity tracking.

antipattern.description: Cycle-time math: define "started" precisely (entered "In Progress" vs. assignee set). LLMs guess inconsistently.
antipattern.reason: Ambiguous definition leads to inconsistent calculations. Pin the definition in the system prompt.

antipattern.description: SAFe terminology overlap (PI vs. Iteration, Feature vs. Story) — pin definitions in the system prompt or get garbage outputs.
antipattern.reason: SAFe reuses Scrum terms with slightly different meanings. Agents hallucinate without explicit vocabulary.

antipattern.description: An agent generating retro themes from comments often surfaces venting, not actionable signal — filter for verbs.
antipattern.reason: Comments capture emotion. Themes need to be actionable improvement statements with owners and due dates.

antipattern.description: Confidence-vote scores look like data but are social signals; do not feed raw to forecasting models.
antipattern.reason: Confidence votes reflect team optimism at PI Planning; they are not predictive of actual delivery. Use historical cycle-time data instead.

antipattern.description: Time-zone math for distributed PI Planning: pass explicit IANA TZ strings, never "PST" / "EST".
antipattern.reason: Daylight saving time rules vary. Agents miscount hours without explicit timezone.

list.item: Throughput + 85th-percentile cycle time beats velocity for forecasting; use Monte Carlo on these for date predictions.
For PI Planning, lock the agenda exactly; teams that "tweak" it lose 30 minutes per ceremony to confusion.
Run replenishment with the PO + tech lead only; opening it to "anyone interested" turns a 30-min meeting into 90.
Make WIP limits a first-class column setting with a hard block (Jira Premium / Linear Cycles) — soft warnings get ignored.
Track I&A actions in the same backlog as features; otherwise they get prioritized to zero.
For remote PI Planning, pre-build the program board the day before; live-creation kills momentum.

template.purpose: Weekly service-delivery review dashboard: throughput, cycle time, WIP vs limits, blockers, aging items.
Compute throughput and cycle-time percentiles from a JSONL of issues with state timestamps.
